output:
# SCARP and RIFT: Recovering Time-Varying Interventional Granger Causal Structure over Latent Regimes with an Exact-Collapse Guarantee to IGC

## 1. Background and Current State of the Art

Granger causality [2] operationalizes causal influence in multivariate time series as predictive improvement: a series x_j Granger-causes x_i if the past of x_j improves the prediction of x_i beyond x_i's own past. The classical linear realization is vector autoregression (VAR) [12], with sparse high-dimensional extensions such as Lasso-Granger [3,4] that impose L1 penalties to recover a sparse lagged adjacency. Because linear VAR cannot capture nonlinear dependencies, a neural lineage emerged: component-wise MLP and LSTM models with sparse input gates (cMLP / cLSTM) [5] read Granger structure off the input-layer weights, the economy statistical recurrent unit eSRU [6] stabilizes this for long lags, and GVAR with self-explaining neural networks [7] recovers both the existence and the SIGN of lagged effects. CUTS and its high-dimensional successor [8,9] bring sparse-regularized causal discovery to irregularly sampled and imputed series.

A parallel constraint-based and dynamics-based lineage targets the same adjacency from different assumptions. PCMCI and PCMCI+ [10] combine conditional-independence testing with a momentary-conditioning step to control for autocorrelation, while DYNOTEARS [11] casts dynamic structure learning as a continuous, acyclicity-constrained optimization extending NOTEARS [27] to time series. Across this entire body of work, a shared and load-bearing assumption is STATIONARITY: the data-generating process, and hence the causal graph, is taken to be fixed over the observation window.

A separate field -- score-based interventional causal discovery -- studies how data collected under INTERVENTIONS sharpen identifiability. GIES [13] generalizes greedy equivalence search to a mix of observational and interventional data and characterizes the interventional (I-)Markov equivalence class, and UT-IGSP [14] extends greedy permutation search to UNKNOWN intervention targets. These methods establish that interventions can collapse an observational equivalence class to a finer I-equivalence class, but they are formulated for iid (non-time-series) data and do not address lagged, time-windowed dependence.

IGC [1] is the synthesis this proposal builds on. Its key observation is that heterogeneous interventional regimes BREAK the stationarity assumption that VAR, PCMCI, neural Granger (cMLP/cLSTM, eSRU), GVAR, DYNOTEARS, and CUTS all rely on, causing those baselines to degrade when observational and interventional data are mixed. IGC turns this liability into leverage with a score-based objective S(G, I) maximized jointly over a graph G and an interventional family I; its Theorem 5.1 / Corollary 5.2 show that, under stated assumptions and small lambda_G, lambda_I, maximizing S(G, I) recovers both the (I*, D_s)-Markov equivalence class of the true graph G* AND the ground-truth interventional family I* -- even WITHOUT interventional target information. Empirically IGC dominates the SOTA set on synthetic linear and nonlinear data (n = 5, 10, 20) and real data on Accuracy / AUROC / F1 / SHD (e.g., linear n=20: SHD 18 vs next-best CUTS 65).

Crucially, IGC relaxes stationarity in ONE specific way -- it allows mechanism shifts at KNOWN, enumerable interventional regimes sharing a SINGLE backbone graph G*. It does not relax the assumption of a single fixed TOPOLOGY, nor does it admit latent, endogenous regime boundaries that are not tied to an intervention label. The authors themselves flag anomaly detection and root-cause analysis as future work -- both of which presuppose that the causal structure can CHANGE over time at points the analyst did not pre-label. This is exactly the seam we open below.

## 2. Key Findings and Analysis

The first finding is a representational impossibility we name SCARP (Static-Causal Aliasing of Regime Partitions). IGC's solution object is a single lagged adjacency G plus an interventional family I indexed by KNOWN membership; neither carries a per-time-step regime label z_t. Therefore IGC is structurally incapable of representing a series whose causal TOPOLOGY changes across contiguous temporal segments with LATENT, endogenous boundaries -- the edge set itself differs across regimes, and the switch points are not signaled by any intervention target. This is not a question of estimator accuracy or hyperparameter tuning: there is literally no slot in (G, I) for a second adjacency, so the best IGC can do is return a single graph that ALIASES the regimes together. Concretely, pooling forms the union/average adjacency, which injects false positives (an edge real only in regime 1 is asserted across the whole series) and false negatives (an edge that flips sign or disappears across regimes is averaged toward zero and pruned). The harm VANISHES precisely at the degenerate condition K = 1 or all-regime adjacencies identical -- the same condition under which the source method is already correct -- which is the signature of a true representational gap rather than a competing heuristic.

The second finding is that SCARP is SILENT under the field's standard evaluation. SHD, AUROC, F1, and Accuracy are scored against ONE assumed ground-truth graph for the whole series; a method recovering the wrong static union can post a deceptively reasonable pooled SHD while being wrong in every individual regime, because per-regime error is never surfaced unless the evaluation is itself segmented. This means the gap is not just unaddressed but UNDER-MEASURED: the very metrics used to crown IGC cannot detect the failure mode, so a regime-aware method and a regime-blind method can look equivalent on pooled metrics while differing sharply on regime-resolved ones. A faithful study must therefore add regime-localization metrics (changepoint detection F1, segmentation error / Hausdorff distance on boundaries) and per-regime SHD alongside the pooled numbers.

The third finding is constructive: the missing capacity is a single axis -- latent temporal segmentation -- and it can be added MINIMALLY on top of IGC via RIFT (Regime-Indexed Factorization of Time-windowed graphs). RIFT keeps IGC's score S(G, I) as a per-segment sub-routine and layers three pieces over it: a latent regime assignment z_t with a boundary-sparsity prior lambda_seg, a shared base graph G_0 with per-regime residual edge gates delta_G^(k) (penalty lambda_G, default zero), and a regime-count fusion penalty lambda_K. Optimization alternates between IGC's own (G, I) recovery WITHIN each segment (inheriting the joint, target-label-free identifiability of Theorem 5.1) and a dynamic-programming / variational update of the segmentation. RIFT thus reuses IGC's estimator wholesale and only decides where time breaks and how far each regime may deviate from the base.

The fourth finding is the rigor anchor: RIFT carries an EXACT-COLLAPSE guarantee. At the default K = 1 with all delta_G^(k) = 0, the segmentation and residual penalties vanish and S_RIFT reduces term-for-term to S_IGC on the full series, emitting IGC's exact graph and family. We define E_collapse as the sup-norm over every edge decision plus every interventional-family membership decision plus the scalar objective gap | S_RIFT - S_IGC |, and require E_collapse <= 1e-6 as a CI-gateable build check: a unit test runs both estimators from the same seed at the default on each IGC dataset and fails the build if the bound is exceeded. This makes RIFT a strict, zero-regression generalization -- under genuine stationarity it IS IGC, decision-for-decision, and any measured improvement is therefore attributable solely to recovering latent regime structure, not to added optimization slack.

Finally, the novelty wedge C1-C4 fixes RIFT's position so that no prior family covers more than two clauses. C1 (time-windowed Granger over multivariate series), C2 (mixed observational + heterogeneous interventional regimes, joint (G, I) recovery without target labels), C3 (time-varying topology over latent endogenous changepoints), and C4 (provable exact-collapse to the base estimator, E_collapse <= 1e-6) jointly pin a point that regime-switching Granger causality (misses C2, C4), CD-NOD / nonstationary causal discovery (misses C2, C4), changepoint / switching-dynamics models (miss C1, C2, C4), and interventional causal discovery / IGC itself (miss C3) all fail to reach. The wedge is the analytic guarantee that RIFT is genuinely new rather than a recombination of an existing line.

## 3. Novel Research Idea (5-Question Formulation)

**Here is a high-level summarized insight of a research field Machine Learning.**

**Here are the five core questions:**

**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

Given that IGC encodes all heterogeneity in a single fixed backbone graph plus an enumerable interventional family indexed by known membership, can we replace that static single-graph representation with a regime-indexed factorization (RIFT) that infers a time-varying causal topology over LATENT, endogenous changepoints -- eliminating the static-causal aliasing (SCARP) that pools regime-specific edges into a wrong union graph -- while PROVABLY collapsing decision-for-decision to IGC at the K=1, zero-residual default with zero regression?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

P1. IGC-class estimators are becoming the emerging standard for recovering causal structure from heterogeneous time series, because they do something most pipelines cannot: jointly recover a Granger causal graph and an interventional family from mixed observational plus interventional data without target labels [1]. This capability sits at the convergence of two large literatures -- time-windowed Granger discovery [2,3,4,5,7] and interventional structure learning [13,14,15] -- so whatever representational assumptions IGC bakes in propagate to every downstream consumer. A blind spot that lives in the representation, not in the tuning, is therefore a systemic risk: forecasting under regime shift, policy evaluation, and closed-loop control all inherit it. SCARP -- the commitment to a single static backbone graph plus a membership-indexed family with no temporal axis -- is exactly such a representational blind spot. Because the field is standardizing on this template [1,11], the cost of leaving SCARP unaddressed compounds across the whole subfield rather than affecting one model. Naming and repairing it early is high-leverage precisely because the template is being widely adopted.

P2. The failure mode SCARP induces is not just harmful but SILENT under the metrics the subfield trusts. Pooled SHD, AUROC, F1, and accuracy are computed against a single assumed static ground-truth graph, so they marginalize over time and cannot see that a regime-blind estimator is wrong in every individual regime [27,11]. A method that recovers the wrong static union-or-average adjacency can still post a respectable pooled score, which means the standard leaderboard rewards exactly the behavior that masks the defect. This mis-measurement is not incidental -- it actively misdirects benchmarking, because authors optimize for the aggregate number that is structurally insensitive to regime-specific error. The broader changepoint and nonstationarity literatures have long warned that time-collapsed evaluation hides temporal structure [20,16], yet causal-discovery benchmarks rarely segment their scoring. Surfacing SCARP therefore also implies a measurement contribution: per-regime evaluation that makes the harm visible. Until the metric sees time, the subfield cannot tell a regime-aware method from a lucky static one.

P3. SCARP repair directly enables the authors' OWN named future work -- anomaly detection and root-cause analysis -- which the IGC paper lists as motivating downstream tasks [1]. Both tasks presuppose that the causal structure can CHANGE at points in time that are not labeled in advance: an anomaly is often precisely a regime in which the causal mechanism differs, and root-cause analysis asks which edges flipped and when [22,28]. A single fixed backbone graph cannot express "the structure here differs from the structure there," so it cannot localize a structural anomaly in time. RIFT supplies the missing representational substrate -- a latent regime-indexed topology -- on which these tasks become well-posed rather than heuristic. By giving each segment its own adjacency while sharing a base graph, RIFT makes "which regime, which edge, which changepoint" a first-class output. This turns the authors' aspirational roadmap into something a method can actually compute.

P4. Importance is amplified by DEPLOYABILITY: RIFT is designed to ship in shadow. By construction it provably reproduces IGC decision-for-decision at the K=1 default, with an exact-collapse bound E_collapse over edge decisions, interventional-family memberships, and score difference held at or below 1e-6 and gated in CI. Exact-collapse discipline is uncommon in causal discovery, where new methods typically replace rather than strictly generalize their predecessors [16,18,8], so practitioners face regression risk on adoption. RIFT removes that risk: a team can adopt it as a strict superset of IGC and turn on the regime machinery only where evidence demands more than one regime. This zero-regression guarantee lowers the barrier to deployment in exactly the high-stakes settings -- forecasting, control, policy -- where IGC is attractive but silent failure is unacceptable. Safety and adoptability, not just accuracy, are what make a representational fix matter in practice. A provable fallback to the trusted baseline is what separates an adoptable contribution from a research prototype.

P5. The contribution generalizes beyond IGC. The lens RIFT introduces -- a shared base graph, a sparse per-regime residual, and a latent segmentation prior, all subject to an exact-collapse guarantee back to a base estimator -- is a general recipe for adding a regime axis to any time-series structure learner. It transfers naturally to neural Granger families [5,6,7,8,9], to constraint-based temporal discovery [10], and to continuous-optimization structure learning [11,27], each of which currently assumes a stationary graph. More broadly it connects to nonstationary causal representation learning [16,17,26], where the same factorization could discipline how latent regimes are introduced without sacrificing a clean reduction to the stationary case. Framed this way, RIFT is not a one-off patch but a reusable design pattern for nonstationary causal discovery. That generality is what gives a single repair outsized expected impact across the field.

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

O1 -- Joint segmentation and causal discovery is a chicken-and-egg, nonconvex coupling. To estimate a per-regime Granger graph you first need the segment boundaries, but to place the boundaries correctly you already need to know where the causal edge set changes. A naive two-stage pipeline (run an off-the-shelf changepoint detector, then run IGC per segment) is provably suboptimal because generic detectors [21,20] fire on shifts in the marginal mean or variance, not on shifts in the lagged causal topology. A regime switch that swaps which parent drives a node while preserving each node's marginal distribution is invisible to such detectors -- exactly the "marginals-silent" switch RIFT must catch. The objective therefore couples a discrete segmentation z_t to a structure-learning score that is itself nonconvex in (G, I), so the loss surface has many shallow basins and the boundary estimate and graph estimate must be solved together, not in sequence.

O2 -- Identifiability under latent regimes with target-free interventions degrades sharply. IGC's identifiability result [1] recovers the (I*, D_s)-Markov equivalence class for a SINGLE backbone graph under a sample-size assumption. Carrying that guarantee to each regime requires every regime to retain enough samples, but partitioning a fixed-length series into K segments shrinks per-regime data roughly by 1/K, inflating both bias and variance of each per-regime estimate and making short regimes nearly unidentifiable. The residual factorization G_0 + delta_G^(k) with partial pooling is precisely what restores statistical strength -- shared edges borrow data across regimes -- but it injects a sharing-versus-separating tension: too much pooling aliases regime differences back into a union graph (re-creating SCARP), too little pooling starves each regime. Choosing lambda_G to sit at the right point on this bias/variance frontier is non-trivial and data-dependent.

O3 -- A topology regime switch and an interventional mechanism shift both break stationarity, and the two confounds are easy to swap. IGC already models interventions (axis C2); RIFT adds latent endogenous regimes (axis C3). A do-style intervention that severs incoming edges to a node looks locally like a new regime, and a genuine regime change in the edge set looks locally like an intervention with unknown target. If the method attributes a known-family intervention to a spurious new regime (or vice versa) it will either over-segment or mask a real topology switch. Disentangling the two requires the score to separate "the interventional family I changed" from "the backbone edge set changed," using the structure of IGC's interventional term while letting the segmentation prior absorb only the endogenous, label-free changepoints.

O4 -- The search is combinatorial along several axes at once: K candidate regimes, a lagged adjacency over n nodes with window length L (so O(n^2 L) parameters per regime), and a segmentation over T timesteps with up to T-1 candidate boundaries. Brute-force joint optimization is exponential in the number of boundaries. Keeping the dynamic-programming segmentation O(K T^2) or better, and keeping the per-regime score maximization tractable, demands explicit penalties (lambda_seg, lambda_K, lambda_G) to bound K, warm-starts that initialize each segment's IGC solve from the shared base G_0, and amortization of repeated score evaluations across candidate boundaries.

O5 -- Exact-collapse through a stochastic pipeline is fragile to engineer. The WEDGE requires that at K=1 with all delta_G^(k)=0, S_RIFT reduces term-for-term to S_IGC and E_collapse <= 1e-6. But IGC's base estimator carries its own randomness -- seeds, initialization, minibatch order, nondeterministic GPU kernels. Guaranteeing a build-test-grade numerical match means the K=1 default path must bypass the segmentation and residual machinery entirely (clamped gates, fixed seed threading, deterministic reductions) so it is byte-for-byte the original IGC computation. This is easy to break silently with any later refactor, hence it must be locked behind a CI collapse gate rather than trusted to discipline.

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

The core reason is structural: each prior line of work owns at most two of the four clauses C1 (time-windowed Granger over multivariate series), C2 (mixed observational plus interventional data with joint, target-label-free (G,I) recovery), C3 (time-varying topology over LATENT endogenous changepoints), and C4 (a provable exact-collapse to the base estimator). The four-way intersection C1+C2+C3+C4 was simply never the target of any single method, and the exact-collapse DISCIPLINE that makes C4 meaningful is itself uncommon in causal discovery, where new methods usually supersede rather than strictly generalize their predecessors. As a result the seam between interventional joint recovery and latent temporal regimes was left open, and no one paid the engineering cost of guaranteeing a strict reduction back to the trusted estimator.

Regime-switching and time-varying Granger causality, including regime-PCMCI and regime-switching VAR [18,12], squarely satisfy C1 and C3: they model multivariate lagged dependence whose structure changes across latent regimes. But they MISS C2 -- they have no notion of interventional heterogeneity or joint recovery of an interventional family, treating all data as observational -- and they MISS C4, offering no exact-collapse guarantee that reduces term-for-term to a base interventional estimator at a single-regime default. They answer "when did the graph change," not "what is the intervention family and does my method strictly generalize IGC."

CD-NOD and the broader nonstationary-heterogeneous causal discovery line [16,17] clearly satisfy C3 and partially C1, modeling distribution shift over time and recovering changing mechanisms. However they MISS C2 in the specific sense IGC requires: they encode nonstationarity through a surrogate index or smooth-change variable rather than a target-free interventional family with recoverable membership [1], so they cannot return the (I*, D_s)-Markov object IGC targets. They also MISS C4 -- there is no exact-collapse contract back to IGC's score. Their nonstationarity is a nuisance variable to be conditioned away, not a regime-indexed causal topology to be recovered.

Changepoint detection and switching linear dynamical systems -- BOCPD, sticky HDP-HMM SLDS, and the kernel changepoint family [21,19,20] -- satisfy C3 with strong latent-segmentation machinery. But they MISS C1 (they segment time series without recovering a lagged multivariate Granger causal graph per segment), MISS C2 (no interventional family at all), and MISS C4 (no base causal estimator to collapse to). They provide exactly the segmentation primitive RIFT borrows, which is why they are the right tool to import, but on their own they say nothing about causal edges or interventions.

Interventional causal discovery, including IGC itself, GIES, and UT-IGSP [1,13,14], is the natural home of C2 -- joint recovery from mixed observational and interventional data, resting on the do-calculus foundations of interventional causal inference [25] -- and IGC additionally satisfies C1 and comes near C4 by virtue of its clean score. But this whole family MISSES C3: it commits to a single fixed topology with no latent temporal segmentation, so it cannot represent an edge set that changes at unlabeled endogenous changepoints. This is the exact seam SCARP names. The estimator is correct within a regime and blind across regimes, and pooling silently aliases regime-specific edges into one static graph.

Invariant causal prediction and causal representation learning from interventions in temporal data [24,26,23] touch environments and interventions, and CITRIS in particular learns causal variables from temporal interventional sequences. Yet they MISS the specific combination at issue: they assume known environments or known intervention timing rather than latent endogenous regimes, they do not perform time-windowed Granger recovery of a lagged adjacency [2,22], and they carry no exact-collapse guarantee to a base estimator. They optimize for invariance or disentanglement, not for a regime-indexed factorization that strictly generalizes IGC.

In sum, only RIFT occupies the C1+C2+C3+C4 intersection: time-windowed Granger recovery, target-label-free joint (G,I) recovery, time-varying topology over latent endogenous changepoints, and a provable exact-collapse to IGC at K=1. The exact-collapse guarantee is what makes the contribution safe and adoptable -- a strict generalization with a zero-regression fallback -- rather than yet another segmentation heuristic competing with the baseline it should extend.

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

(a) METHOD detail. RIFT (Regime-Indexed Factorization of Time-windowed graphs) sits on top of IGC's score S_IGC(G, I). We add three ingredients. (i) A latent regime assignment z_t in {1..K} over time, with a boundary-sparsity / fusion prior penalizing the number of changepoints by lambda_seg. (ii) A shared base graph G_0 plus per-regime residual edge gates delta_G^(k), penalized by lambda_G (default 0), so each regime's effective backbone is G_0 (+) delta_G^(k) where (+) toggles edges; with lambda_G driving most deltas to zero the model defaults to partial pooling around G_0. (iii) A regime-count penalty lambda_K. The objective is

S_RIFT = sum_k S_IGC(G_0 (+) delta_G^(k), I | segment k) - lambda_seg * (#changepoints) - lambda_K * K - lambda_G * sum_k || delta_G^(k) ||.

Optimization alternates: (a) fix z and run IGC's own score-based (G, I) recovery independently per segment, warm-started from G_0; (b) fix the graphs and update z by dynamic-programming / variational segmentation over T using the per-segment IGC score as the emission cost. Hyperparameters: K_max (search cap on regimes), lambda_seg (boundary penalty), lambda_K (regime-count penalty), lambda_G (residual-gate penalty). EXACT-COLLAPSE default: K=1, delta_G=0 makes S_RIFT equal S_IGC term-for-term on the full series.

(b) DATASETS. For back-compatibility we reuse IGC's synthetic LINEAR and NON-LINEAR generators at n = 5, 10, 20 nodes and IGC's real-world dataset, so the original Accuracy / AUROC / F1 / SHD table reproduces under RIFT at its K=1 default. We then ADD non-stationary variants: piecewise series with K = 2..4 latent regimes whose adjacency EDGE SETS differ, with changepoints at UNLABELED times that are NOT aligned to any interventional regime; we control the per-regime edge-set divergence (Hamming distance between adjacencies) and include marginals-silent switches (parent swaps that preserve each node's stationary marginal). We add a MIXED condition where interventional regimes and latent topology switches co-occur to stress the C2-vs-C3 confound (O3). For real non-stationary structure with known regimes we propose: task-block fMRI / neural recordings with externally timed cognitive blocks (e.g., the NetSim-style simulated-and-real fMRI benchmarks [29]), financial regime data with documented volatility / crisis regimes (e.g., daily index and sector returns spanning a known crisis boundary), and climate regime indices (e.g., ENSO / Nino3.4-indexed teleconnection series) where regime labels are externally available for changepoint scoring.

(c) METRICS. We report POOLED Accuracy / AUROC / F1 / SHD (back-compat with IGC's table) PLUS regime-resolved metrics: per-regime SHD against each true regime adjacency; changepoint-detection F1 and boundary error measured as Hausdorff distance and mean absolute segmentation error (in timesteps) between predicted and true boundaries; number-of-regimes recovery (predicted K vs true K, with absolute error and a hit-rate); and E_collapse, the supremum over edge decisions, interventional-family memberships, and |S_RIFT - S_IGC|, which must be <= 1e-6 at the default.

(d) BASELINES. IGC itself, run regime-blind on the pooled series, is the central foil (it must exhibit SCARP -- an aliased union/average graph). Regime-naive re-fits of the SOTA set {VAR, PCMCI, NGC (cMLP/cLSTM), eSRU, DyNoTears, GVAR, CUTS} run on the pooled series. A 2-STAGE baseline: off-the-shelf changepoint detection (BOCPD [21]; or ruptures [20]) THEN per-segment IGC -- this isolates the value of JOINT optimization over sequential. An ORACLE-SEGMENTATION upper bound: RIFT given the true boundaries, fitting per-regime graphs with no segmentation error, bounding how much of any gap is segmentation versus estimation.

(e) ABLATIONS. (1) Shared-base-only (delta_G^(k) forced 0) vs +residual gates, isolating the value of regime-specific residuals. (2) Joint RIFT vs the 2-stage pipeline, isolating joint optimization. (3) lambda_seg sweep to map the under-segmentation (missed switches) to over-segmentation (spurious regimes) trade-off. (4) Partial pooling (G_0 + small delta) vs fully independent per-regime graphs, isolating statistical-strength borrowing (O2). (5) With vs without the exact-collapse clamp, confirming the clamp is what secures E_collapse.

(f) FALSIFIERS / placebos. On a TRULY stationary series (single regime), RIFT must recover K=1 and match IGC within noise; if RIFT manufactures spurious regimes or beats IGC here, the central claim is FALSIFIED. A boundary-SHUFFLE placebo permutes the time index to destroy temporal regime structure while preserving marginals; RIFT's regime-resolved advantage must collapse to the pooling baseline -- if the "advantage" survives shuffling, it was an artifact, not real regime recovery.

(g) EXPECTED OUTCOMES (quantitative HYPOTHESES with ranges, not results). On non-stationary synthetic data, we hypothesize RIFT cuts per-regime SHD by roughly 30-60% relative to regime-blind IGC while matching pooled Accuracy / AUROC / F1 / SHD within noise (no back-compat regression). Changepoint-detection F1 in roughly 0.80-0.95 when per-regime edge-set divergence is large enough (Hamming separation above a few edges); degrading gracefully toward chance as divergence shrinks to zero. E_collapse <= 1e-6 verified at the K=1 default. The 2-stage baseline trails joint RIFT, with the gap widest on marginals-silent switches where generic detectors are blind. Gains scale monotonically with K and with cross-regime edge-set divergence, and vanish at K=1 (the harm is SILENT under pooled metrics, surfacing only in regime-resolved metrics).

(h) CI COLLAPSE GATE. A build-blocking unit test instantiates RIFT at K=1 with delta_G=0 on EVERY IGC dataset (linear, non-linear at n=5,10,20, and the real dataset) and fails the build if E_collapse > 1e-6, guaranteeing the default path is numerically identical to upstream IGC.

(i) Responsible-AI and deployment safeguards. A responsible-AI pass (advisory verdict YELLOW, no blocking issues) folds seven guardrails into the design rather than bolting them on. (1) Causal-claim humility: RIFT's per-regime graphs are Granger-predictive / interventional-observational structures, not certified mechanisms, so root-cause and anomaly outputs are decision-support requiring human-in-the-loop validation before consequential action in medical, financial, infrastructure, or policy settings. (2) Calibrated uncertainty and abstention: report posterior uncertainty on regime-boundary timestamps and per-regime edge gates, and abstain from regime attribution when boundary evidence falls below a confidence threshold, so confidently wrong labels do not propagate downstream. (3) Spurious-regime guard: the K=1 exact-collapse falsifier (E_collapse <= 1e-6) and the boundary-shuffle placebo are mandatory model-selection controls against over-segmentation that could manufacture false regimes. (4) Disaggregated evaluation: when applied to data spanning populations (clinical or user cohorts, geographically heterogeneous sensor networks), evaluate regime assignments and attributions per subgroup so the model does not learn spurious regimes that proxy group membership. (5) Data governance and provenance: use all real datasets (neuro/fMRI, financial, climate) under their licenses and consent terms, document provenance and anonymization, and confirm no re-identification risk from regime labels. (6) Dual-use and blame attribution: automated per-regime root-cause attributions could be misused to assign blame, so any consequential deployment must provide contestability -- audit logs, override paths, and human review. (7) Reproducibility as a safety property: surface the exact-collapse CI gate as an auditable responsible-engineering control in the replication package so reviewers can verify zero-regression against IGC without retraining.

## 4. References

[1] Zhang, K., et al. "Learning Flexible Time-windowed Granger Causality Integrating Heterogeneous Interventional Time Series Data." Proceedings of the 30th ACM SIGKDD Conference on Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining (KDD), 2024.
[2] Granger, C. W. J. "Investigating Causal Relations by Econometric Models and Cross-spectral Methods." Econometrica, 37(3):424-438, 1969.
[3] Arnold, A., Liu, Y., and Abe, N. "Temporal Causal Modeling with Graphical Granger Methods." Proceedings of the 13th ACM SIGKDD Conference on Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining (KDD), 2007.
[4] Bahadori, M. T., and Liu, Y. "An Examination of Practical Granger Causality Inference." Proceedings of the SIAM International Conference on Data Mining (SDM), 2013.
[5] Tank, A., Covert, I., Foti, N., Shojaie, A., and Fox, E. "Neural Granger Causality." IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence (TPAMI), 44(8):4267-4279, 2021.
[6] Khanna, S., and Tan, V. Y. F. "Economy Statistical Recurrent Units for Inferring Nonlinear Granger Causality." International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR), 2020.
[7] Marcinkevics, R., and Vogt, J. E. "Interpretable Models for Granger Causality Using Self-explaining Neural Networks (GVAR)." International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR), 2021.
[8] Cheng, Y., Yang, R., Xiao, T., Li, Z., Suo, J., He, K., and Dai, Q. "CUTS: Neural Causal Discovery from Irregular Time-series Data." International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR), 2023.
